title: practical english usage - Part 181
content:
if  I were,      etc 232 subjunctive:       that     she    go,   that     they     be, For tenses in indirect speech, ►    259.

You'll work with Mr Harris, who will explain everything to you.

I'll come to the opera with you, although I probably won't enjoy it.

I won't see you next week because I'll be in Canada.

as (meaning 'because'), or to non-identifying relative clauses (►  234).

These rules do not usually apply to clauses beginning because, although, since or 8  exceptions or (   ... at the same time as he's working...) He's working. But at the same time as he works, he's exercising.

Progressives are often replaced by simple forms in subordinate clauses.

(More natural than ... while he had been driving.) He probably crashed because he had gone to sleep while he was driving.

For thirty years, he had done no more than he (had) needed to.

I've usually liked the people l('ve) worked with.

It's been a good time while it('s) lasted.

present perfect and past perfect tenses, if the meaning is clear.

Simple past verb forms are used quite often in subordinate clauses instead of 7 simplification          of  perfect      and   progressive         verbs For past tenses after Its time, ►    502; after I'd rather, ►    566; after I wish, ►    632.

or not.

I would always try to help anybody who was in trouble, whether I knew them not (     .. .-what yeu-would-think:) In a perfect world, you would be able to say exactly what you thought.

Would you follow me wherever I went? (     .. .-wherever ! would go?) not (     Jf4 would have... who would ask for it.) If I had lots of money, 1 would give some to anybody who asked for it.

verbs. This happens in //-clauses (►   239), and also after most other conjunctions.

Would, like will, is avoided in subordinate clauses; instead, we  generally use past 6 past    instead      of  would      ...

Conjunctions, Sentences and ClausesSection 20 grammar •  232   subjunctive: that she go, that they be, if I were, etc or (   ... that James contacted ...) It was important that James should contact Adam as soon as possible.

(   .. . that every child has ...) It is essential that every child should have the same educational opportunities.

or ordinary present and past tenses.

In that-c lauses, British people usually prefer should + infinitive (►   264.7), Most subjunctive structures are formal and unusual in British English.

5  other     structures If we have to pay £2,000,  then so be it. (= We   can’ t do anything to change it.) Be that as it may.. . (= Whether that is true or not...) He's a sort of adopted uncle, as it were. (= ... in a way.) God bless you.     Heaven forbid.

God save the Queen! Long live the King!

Subjunctives are also used in certain fixed phrases. Examples: 4 fixed    phrases:     as  it  were;     so   be   it If I were you I should stop smoking. I wish it were Saturday.

a formal style, are also subjunctives.

I were and he/she/it were, used for example after if (►   239.4) and wish (►   632) in The Director asked that he be allowed to advertise for more staff.

It is important that Ella be present when we sign the papers.

Be has special subjunctive forms: I be, you be, etc.

I recommended that you move to another office.

present-tense verbs (but they may refer to the past).

With verbs that are not third person singular, the forms are the same as ordinary We felt it desirable that he not leave school before eighteen.

Do is not used in negative subjunctives. Note the word order.

The judge recommended that Simmons remain in prison for life.

Our  advice is that the company invest in new equipment.

It was important that James contact Adam as soon as possible.

It is essential that every child have the same educational opportunities.

sentences.

essential, important, advice). The  same forms are used in both present and past something is important or desirable (e.g. suggest, recommend, ask, insist, vital, especially in American English, after words which  express the idea that with no -(e)s (e.g. she see).
